# Your first extension {#sec:extensions}

An extension is a TypeScript or JavaScript module that Pi loads at runtime with
`jiti`. Jiti resolves the module and transpiles TypeScript syntax on demand, so
an extension does not need a separate build step; it does not bundle or
type-check the code. The module's default export is a **factory**: a function
that receives `ExtensionAPI` and registers the extension's behavior. The factory
registers hooks, tools, commands, shortcuts, flags,
renderers, and providers, which Pi invokes at defined points in its runtime.

Extensions execute with the permissions of the Pi process. They are trusted
code, not a sandbox. Review a package before installing it and treat an
extension's filesystem, process, network, and credential access as you would a
normal local program.

A **lifecycle** is the ordered sequence of events Pi emits while it loads an
extension, runs a session, processes turns, and shuts the session down. An
event is a named occurrence in that sequence; a hook is the extension handler
registered to observe or affect that occurrence. The examples below use the
public `ExtensionAPI` contract rather than internal implementation details.

## What an extension is

An **extension** is code Pi loads at runtime. A **hook** is a named event where
that code can observe or change a supported part of the lifecycle. A **tool**
is a model-callable function with a validated input shape. A **context** is the
object passed to a handler, including the session, cancellation signal, and
possible UI.

Begin with a progressive one-file example rather than copying the full package
example. First register one command that displays a notice. Then add a tool,
then a `session_start` hook, and finally durable state. The complete example
below shows all patterns together; read it once for each stage instead of
copying every feature at once.

## Choose the right customization mechanism

Before writing code, read [Which mechanism do you need?](04-customizing-pi.qmd#which-mechanism-do-you-need). An extension is powerful, but do not use one to recreate or patch Pi internals as an unreviewed fork. Favor public `ExtensionAPI` methods over importing coding-agent internals.

## Discovery, trust, and loading

Pi automatically discovers a single-file extension or directory `index.ts` in
these places:

| Scope | Files discovered |
|---|---|
| Global | `~/.pi/agent/extensions/*.ts`, `~/.pi/agent/extensions/*/index.ts` |
| Project | `.pi/extensions/*.ts`, `.pi/extensions/*/index.ts` |

Project-local resources are loaded only after the project is trusted. You can
also add local paths in `settings.json`, or try a path for one invocation with
`pi -e ./path/to/extension.ts`. The latter is ideal for a quick test, but
`/reload` hot reload is intended for auto-discovered locations.

At startup, the resource loader loads extension factories before session start.
An async factory is awaited before startup continues. That makes it suitable
for one-time initialization such as discovering available AI models and calling
`pi.registerProvider()`. Do not start timers, watchers, processes, or sockets
in the factory. For example, this command loads extension factories to discover
available AI models from providers but does not start a session:

```bash
pi --list-models
```

Start long-lived session resources in `session_start` and close them in a way
that is safe to call more than once (idempotently) in `session_shutdown`.

## The lifecycle you can rely on

In this diagram, a **factory** is the default-exported function that registers
an extension; **session** means one loaded Pi conversation; a **turn** is one
model request and its resulting tool work; a **provider hook** observes the
provider-specific request boundary; and **tool execution hooks** surround a
model-callable tool. Arrows mean “happens before,” not that every internal
event is shown.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-extension-lifecycle
%%| fig-cap: "Representative extension event flow (not exhaustive)."
flowchart TD
  factory[Factory runs] --> trust[project_trust]
  trust --> start[session_start]
  start --> resources[resources_discover]
  resources --> input[Prompt and input]
  input --> agent[before_agent_start]
  agent --> turn[Turns, streaming, tools]
  turn --> settled[agent_end and agent_settled]
  settled --> shutdown[session_shutdown]
```

The diagram omits message-level events and detailed branches for session
operations; the table below includes some representative events omitted from
the diagram. For the complete event sequence, return values, and parallel-tool
ordering, see `packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md`; use
`packages/coding-agent/src/core/extensions/types.ts` for the exact types.

### Event groups and common use

Read the event-group labels in plain language: **Trust/resources** decides what
may load; **Session** opens, closes, or rebuilds session state;
**Input/agent** turns user input into agent work; **Turn/messages** covers
context and streaming; **Provider** is the serialized request boundary;
**Tool** gates and observes tool calls; **AI model** tracks model choices; and
**User shell** is the `!`/`!!` command boundary.

| Event group | Representative events | Use it for |
|---|---|---|
| Trust/resources | `project_trust`, `resources_discover` | Decide project trust or contribute paths for skills/prompts/themes |
| Session | `session_start`, `session_shutdown`, `session_before_switch`, `session_before_compact` | Initialize/clean resources, persist/rebuild state, gate a session operation |
| Input/agent | `input`, `before_agent_start`, `agent_start`, `agent_end`, `agent_settled` | Transform input, add prompt context, track the true idle state |
| Turn/messages | `turn_start`, `context`, `message_update`, `turn_end` | Adjust context or render/measure streaming activity |
| Provider | `before_provider_headers`, `before_provider_request`, `after_provider_response` | Tracing, a request rewrite, response metadata |
| Tool | `tool_execution_start`, `tool_call`, `tool_execution_update`, `tool_result`, `tool_execution_end` | Observe progress, block/mutate calls, patch results |
| AI model | `model_select`, `thinking_level_select` | Update UI/state for the selected AI model |
| User shell | `user_bash` | Route `!` and `!!` through a different execution backend |

`tool_call` is a policy enforcement point: it runs after schema validation and
can block a tool.

Its input is mutable. A change affects execution and is visible to later
`tool_call` handlers. **Pi does not revalidate input after a handler mutates
it.** Keep mutations narrow and validate every invariant your change could
violate.

`context` receives a deep copy of messages that an extension may replace. It
is a safer place to prune/inject LLM context than modifying session storage.
`before_provider_request` is lower-level still: it sees the provider-specific
payload after serialization, so changes there are not reflected by
`ctx.getSystemPrompt()`.

## Runnable: canonical staged first extension {#a-complete-first-extension}

This is the chapter's single canonical extension example. It is intentionally
small enough to run first as a command, then as a tool, and finally as a
lifecycle hook; the listing shows the three stages together so later sections
can refer back to one source. It adds a `hello` tool callable by an AI model, a
`/hello-pi` command, and a startup notification.

> **Runnable**
>
> - **Filename/location:** `~/.pi/agent/extensions/hello-pi.ts`
> - **Command:** start `pi` in any project, then run `/hello-pi Ada`
> - **Expected result:** startup shows `hello-pi extension is ready`; the command shows `Hello, Ada!`. The model can also call the `hello` tool.
> - **Reload:** edit the file and run `/reload`; the new `session_start` notification confirms the reloaded factory.
> - **Cleanup:** remove `~/.pi/agent/extensions/hello-pi.ts` when finished, then run `/reload` or restart Pi.
> - **Permissions caution:** this file is trusted code with the same filesystem, process, network, and credential permissions as Pi. Do not run an extension you have not reviewed.

1. Create the file in an auto-discovered global location:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.pi/agent/extensions
```

2. Save this as `~/.pi/agent/extensions/hello-pi.ts`:

```ts
import { Type } from "@earendil-works/pi-ai";
import { defineTool, type ExtensionAPI } from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent";

const helloTool = defineTool({
  name: "hello",
  label: "Hello",
  description: "Greet a person by name",
  parameters: Type.Object({
    name: Type.String({ description: "The person to greet" }),
  }),
  async execute(_toolCallId, params, _signal, _onUpdate, _ctx) {
    return {
      content: [{ type: "text", text: `Hello, ${params.name}!` }],
      details: { greeted: params.name },
    };
  },
});

export default function register(pi: ExtensionAPI) {
  pi.registerTool(helloTool);

  pi.registerCommand("hello-pi", {
    description: "Show a greeting notification",
    handler: async (args, ctx) => {
      ctx.ui.notify(`Hello, ${args || "world"}!`, "info");
    },
  });

  pi.on("session_start", async (_event, ctx) => {
    ctx.ui.notify("hello-pi extension is ready", "info");
  });
}
```

The tool definition uses `defineTool`, as does the repository's verified
extension example:
`packages/coding-agent/examples/extensions/hello.ts`.
`Type.Object` is a runtime schema. `defineTool` derives the static type of
`params`, and the agent loop validates AI-model-supplied arguments against that
schema before calling `execute`.

3. Start Pi in any project. The startup notification confirms discovery.
   Run `/hello-pi Ada` to exercise the command. Ask Pi to greet Ada with the
   `hello` tool, or inspect its active tool list, to exercise the interface
   exposed to the AI model.

4. Test as a user: edit the discovered file and run `/reload` in Pi. That path
   emits `session_shutdown`, reloads resources, then emits `session_start` with
   `reason: "reload"`.

   Separate rule for extension authors: if a *command handler* itself calls
   `ctx.reload()`, end the handler there. Reloading tears down the current
   runtime and spins up a new one, but the old `await` resumes in the pre-reload
   call frame with the old `ctx` and closed-over state:

   ```ts
   // Author responsibility: do not resume work on the pre-reload instance.
   await ctx.reload();
   return;
   ```

   Put any post-reload behavior in the newly loaded factory (for example a
   `session_start` handler), not after `await ctx.reload()`.

For a one-off test outside discovery, use `pi -e ~/.pi/agent/extensions/hello-pi.ts`.
The global file is preferable when testing `/reload`.

## What `ExtensionAPI` and context provide

`ExtensionAPI` is the registration/control surface. Its important methods are:

| API | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `pi.on()` | Subscribe to a typed lifecycle event |
| `pi.registerTool()` | Add or override a model-callable tool |
| `pi.registerCommand()` | Add `/name` command with optional argument completion |
| `pi.registerShortcut()` / `pi.registerFlag()` | Add a keybinding action or CLI option |
| `pi.sendMessage()` / `pi.sendUserMessage()` | Put a custom or user message into agent flow |
| `pi.appendEntry()` | Persist extension-only session data outside LLM context |
| `pi.registerMessageRenderer()` / `pi.registerEntryRenderer()` | Render custom message/entry types in the transcript |
| `pi.getActiveTools()` / `pi.setActiveTools()` | Inspect or change the currently exposed tool set |
| `pi.setModel()` / `pi.setThinkingLevel()` | Change live session AI-model settings |
| `pi.registerProvider()` / `pi.unregisterProvider()` | Register an endpoint/provider for AI models at runtime |
| `pi.exec()` | Run a process with Pi's helper interface |

Handlers receive a context. The most useful fields are `ctx.cwd`, `ctx.mode`,
`ctx.hasUI`, `ctx.sessionManager`, `ctx.modelRegistry`, `ctx.model`, `ctx.signal`,
and `ctx.ui`. Pass `ctx.signal` to cancelable nested work. Check
`ctx.mode === "tui"` for terminal-only behavior and `ctx.hasUI` before prompting.

`ExtensionCommandContext` adds session-changing methods such as `newSession`,
`fork`, `navigateTree`, `switchSession`, `waitForIdle`, and `reload`. They are
available only in command handlers because changing a session from an ordinary
event handler can deadlock or leave stale state.

## Debugging extensions

Test a one-off file with `pi -e ./ext.ts`; use `/reload` for an auto-discovered file. Errors appear in Pi output and logs. See [Testing, debugging, and contribution](#sec:testing) for the wider workflow.

Work from loading toward the failing behavior:

1. **Confirm the module loads.** In TUI mode, add a `session_start`
   notification. Then load the file directly with
   `pi -e path/to/extension.ts`; if that works, discovery is the problem.
   Check the exact filename (`extensions/name.ts` or
   `extensions/name/index.ts`), the default factory export, and project trust
   for `.pi/extensions/`. A named export alone is not loaded.
2. **Check the code independently.** Run
   `npx tsc --noEmit path/to/extension.ts`. Jiti transpiles TypeScript but does
   not type-check it, so type errors can survive loading and fail later.
3. **Isolate one boundary.** Exercise one command or one simple tool call. Add
   temporary notifications to the relevant hooks in TUI mode, or concise
   logging when testing print mode. Check command names, tool names, schemas,
   and descriptions before debugging a multi-turn interaction.
4. **Inspect model-facing state.** The built-in `/debug` command writes rendered
   TUI lines and the latest LLM messages to `~/.pi/agent/pi-debug.log`, which shows
   whether tool definitions and prompt metadata reached the model.
5. **Exercise lifecycle edges.** Run `/reload`, then test a new session,
   resume, fork, and exit when relevant. Verify `session_shutdown` cleanup and
   reconstruction in `session_start`.
6. **Test without a TUI.** Run `pi -e path/to/extension.ts -p "your prompt"` to
   catch unguarded UI assumptions. Notifications do nothing in print mode, so
   do not use their absence there as evidence that loading failed.

### Common pitfalls

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Extension never loads | Wrong path, missing default export, or untrusted project | Check discovery conventions, export the factory as default, and establish project trust |
| `tool_call` handler does not match | Tool name or type-narrowing check is wrong | Check `event.toolName` and `isToolCallEventType()` |
| State disappears after `/reload` | State exists only in closed-over variables | Rebuild it from session entries in `session_start` |
| RPC dialog waits indefinitely | The client does not answer `extension_ui_request` | Send the matching response or set a dialog timeout |
| A dialog silently denies in print/JSON mode | `ctx.hasUI` was not checked | Define a noninteractive policy before calling the dialog |
| Extension works once but fails after reload | Stale references or leaked resources | Clean up safely in `session_shutdown`; do not reuse the old context after `reload()` |
| Model never calls the tool | Weak description or missing prompt guidance | Tighten `description` and add tool-named `promptGuidelines` |
| Parallel tools overwrite a file | The mutation bypasses Pi's per-file queue | Wrap the entire read-modify-write operation in `withFileMutationQueue` |

Pi logs ordinary handler errors and continues. `tool_call` is fail-safe: if one
of its handlers throws, Pi stops running later `tool_call` handlers for that
call, does not execute the tool, and does not run `tool_result` handlers for it.
Instead, the thrown error message becomes an `isError: true` tool-result message
stored in the session and sent to the model; the agent loop and sibling tool
calls can continue. Test policy hooks on both allow and deny paths.

## Before deployment

The runnable example is a demo, not a production safety review. Before
shipping an extension, continue with [Extension patterns: basic to advanced](#sec:customization)
for safety gates, noninteractive behavior, durable state reconstruction,
same-file mutation queues, concurrency, and the verification ladder.
